one_line_takeaway: A conceptual framework arguing that governing AI-agent ecosystems requires infrastructure external to the agents (attribution, interaction-shaping, and response tooling) analogous to internet protocols, delivered as a nine-item catalog of research directions rather than any implemented or evaluated system.
key_finding: There is no empirical finding; the paper's central claim/argument is that system-level interventions (instruction-following training, adversarial robustness, prompting) are useful but insufficient to govern how heterogeneous agents interact with each other, with institutions, and with other actors, so external protocols and systems - agent infrastructure - will be as indispensable to agent ecosystems as HTTPS-class protocols are to the Internet. The concrete deliverable is the three-function taxonomy and the catalog of nine infrastructure types with their use cases, adoption paths, and open questions. A recurring cross-cutting argument is that infrastructure requiring multi-party coordination (communication protocols, agent IDs) is gated by network effects and interoperability, whereas single-adopter infrastructure (oversight layers, rollbacks) is immediately useful even at low adoption.
core_contribution: The paper proposes and develops the concept of "agent infrastructure": technical systems and shared protocols EXTERNAL to AI agents that mediate and influence their interactions with their environments, positioned as complementary to "system-level interventions" that modify the agent itself (training, prompting, unlearning, filtering data). Its contribution is a unifying conceptual framework plus a structured catalog, not any implemented system or empirical result. The framework organizes agent infrastructure into three functions, each with concrete infrastructure types: (1) Attribution - identity binding, certification, agent IDs; (2) Interaction - agent channels, oversight layers, inter-agent communication, commitment devices; (3) Response - incident reporting, rollbacks. For each of these nine infrastructure types the paper supplies a description, potential functions, existing alternatives, adoption analysis, limitations, and open research questions. The authors explicitly frame their contribution against prior work (Section 7): they build on classic multi-agent protocol work (Kahn & Cerf 1988; Wooldridge 2009) and recent agent-governance work (Chan et al. 2023/2024; Shavit et al. 2023; Hammond et al. 2025; Perrier), but claim novelty in accounting for foundation-model-based agents, grounding the catalog in modern internet infrastructure, and taking a broader view of infrastructure functions than the visibility-focused prior work of Chan et al. (2024a;b).
threat_model: The paper is a governance/position paper but engages substantively and explicitly with security threat models rather than inventing one post hoc. Section 2.3 states that different infrastructure serves different threat models: inter-agent communication protocols and commitment devices address cooperation problems between agents; oversight layers and rollbacks address misuse or misalignment; identity binding and agent IDs address both cooperation and harms. Concrete adversary scenarios drawn from the paper's own text include: Sybil attacks (creating many fake identities to undermine a network, e.g. the cited Spotify streaming-fraud case), agent-enabled scams and VOIP fraud, indirect prompt injection to drain a bank account or hijack an agent, jailbreaking one agent to act on an attacker's behalf (e.g. financial transfers), language-model/text-image "worms" that self-propagate along inter-agent communication channels (Cohen et al. 2024; Gu et al. 2024), ID theft/spoofing and compromise of certificate authorities, and abuse of incident-reporting systems to distract regulators with spurious reports. The paper's stance is that external infrastructure gives leverage points (restricting an agent's interactions restricts its potential negative impact) that behavioural modification alone cannot provide.
limitations: The paper is explicit about its own limitations (Sections 2.3, 6, 8). It takes no stance on prioritization among infrastructure types. The catalog is stated to be incomplete/non-exhaustive. Adoption is the central challenge: coordination-dependent infrastructure faces network effects that advantage incumbents (e.g. Google's enterprise partners for its A2A protocol) over smaller players with superior tech (6.1); infrastructure from different developers may be mutually non-interoperable and dominant players may exploit this to lock out competitors (6.2); and widespread adoption of insecure infrastructure can be hard to reverse (lock-in), illustrated by the slow rollout of verifiable BGP after the 2008 Pakistani-ISP incident that took down YouTube worldwide (6.3). Individual infrastructure types carry their own risks the paper flags: certification can create a false sense of security or suffer adverse selection (the TrustARC/TRUSTe case, where certified sites were found LESS trustworthy); agent IDs let attackers target high-value agents and users; identity binding threatens anonymity, whistleblowing, and personal safety; rollbacks create moral hazard. Finally, the authors stress agent infrastructure is a "platform for policies and norms," not a complete solution - it does not by itself resolve liability allocation or downstream harms like unemployment.
